intro: C++ expands on C by introducing high-level abstractions like object-oriented programming, classes, template metaprogramming, and RAII. Downscaling from C++ back to C means removing these complex abstractions to focus on a minimal, procedural subset that maps directly to target assembly instructions. Developers convert C++ assets to C to eliminate heavy runtime dependencies, simplify ABI compilation targets, and ensure compatibility with legacy or micro-embedded systems.

diffs.from: Static nominal typing supported by function overloading, explicit template parameters, and class hierarchies.
diffs.to: Static, procedural type system relying strictly on fundamental types, basic structs, and generic void pointers.

diffs.from: Rich, multi-paradigm layout utilizing namespaces, classes, templates, and reference tokens (`&`).
diffs.to: Minimalist procedural syntax centered entirely on functions, files, and explicit pointer markers (`*`).

diffs.from: Automated allocation bounds tracked through class scopes, constructors, destructors, and smart pointer wrappers.
diffs.to: Explicit application-driven allocation routines using manual heap allocators like `malloc` and `free`.

diffs.from: Standard abstractions like `std::thread`, mutex frameworks, futures, and modern coroutine pipelines.
diffs.to: No native concurrency primitives; developers must rely directly on underlying platform interfaces like `pthreads`.

diffs.from: The vast STL, providing comprehensive template collections, string streams, and advanced algorithms.
diffs.to: The minimal standard C library, focused primarily on low-level string arrays, file I/O operations, and system macros.

examples.title: Polymorphic Structures
examples.description: Replacing C++ class virtual function tables with explicit function pointers inside a traditional C struct.

examples.title: Memory Block Allocation
examples.description: Replacing modern type-safe operators with explicit byte-sized dynamic allocations.

tips.title: Implement Explicit Resource Cleanup Routines
tips.body: C has no built-in destructors. You must write explicit initialization and cleanup functions (e.g., `init_obj`, `free_obj`) for your custom structs, and remember to call them manually.

tips.title: Mangle Function Names to Replace Overloading
tips.body: C does not allow multiple functions to share the same name with different signatures. You must differentiate them by choosing unique names, such as `print_int` and `print_float`.

tips.title: Pass Explicit Pointers to Simulate Context
tips.body: Since C structs cannot contain internal methods, you must pass an explicit pointer to the target struct (often named `self` or `this`) as the first argument of your functions.

tips.title: Use Explicit Error Codes Instead of Exceptions
tips.body: C does not support `try/catch` exceptions. Instead, have functions return integer error codes or boolean flags, and verify those return statuses immediately after every invocation.
